name: Guadalupe Viaduct from Cañamero via the Isabel la Católica Trail (PR-CC 242)
name_original: Viaducto de Guadalupe desde Cañamero por el Camino de Isabel la Católica (PR-CC 242)
region_intro: Hiking around Cañamero offers exploration within the Villuercas-Ibores-Jara UNESCO Global Geopark, an area known for its ancient geological formations and "apalachense" landscape. The region features a diverse terrain of mountains, valleys, and rock formations, some dating back 300 million years. Key natural features include the Ruecas River, which carves a canyon through the landscape, and the Cancho del Fresno Reservoir. This environment provides varied hiking opportunities across its unique geological and natural settings. Best hiking trails around Cañamero The most popular hiking route is Embalse del Cancho del Fresno y Cueva la Chiquita — circular desde Cañamero, a 2.6 miles (4.1 km) trail that takes 1 hour 11 minutes to complete. This route explores the reservoir area and passes near the historically significant La Chiquita Cave. Another top favourite among local hikers is Guadalupe Viaduct from Cañamero via the Isabel la Católica Trail (PR-CC 242), a difficult 17.3 miles (27.9 km) path. This extensive trail offers a challenging experience through the regional landscape. Local hikers also love the Walk around the Embalse del Cancho del Fresno from Cañamero, a 9.3 miles (15.0 km) trail leading through the Cancho del Fresno Reservoir area, often completed in about 4 hours 37 minutes. Hiking around Cañamero is defined by its ancient geological formations, river canyons, and reservoir landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate walks to more challenging routes. The routes in Cañamero are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 230 hikers have used komoot to explore Cañamero's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Cañamero?
A: There are around 10 hiking trails in the Cañamero area, offering a range of experiences within the Villuercas-Ibores-Jara UNESCO Global Geopark. Most routes are moderate, with some challenging options for experienced hikers.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in Cañamero?
A: Yes, Cañamero offers several circular hiking routes. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1272865170/embalse-del-cancho-del-fresno-y-cueva-la-chiquita-circular-desde-canamero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Cancho del Fresno y Cueva la Chiquita — circular desde Cañamero&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 4.1 km trail exploring the reservoir and passing near the historic La Chiquita Cave. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1272868178/cancho-del-fresno-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cancho del Fresno Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 14.3 km moderate route.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes around Cañamero?
A: The terrain around Cañamero is diverse, characterized by the ancient geological formations of the Villuercas-Ibores-Jara UNESCO Global Geopark. You'll find mountains, valleys, and rock formations, some dating back 300 million years. Trails often feature river canyons, such as along the Ruecas River, and reservoir landscapes, providing varied elevation changes and scenic views.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Cañamero?
A: While most routes are moderate, the region offers natural beauty that can be enjoyed by families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1272865170/embalse-del-cancho-del-fresno-y-cueva-la-chiquita-circular-desde-canamero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Cancho del Fresno y Cueva la Chiquita — circular desde Cañamero&lt;/a&gt; is a shorter, moderate trail that might be suitable for families looking for a less strenuous walk, exploring the reservoir and a cave.

Q: What are some notable natural features or viewpoints to see while hiking?
A: Cañamero is rich in natural features. You can hike to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3406495" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Villuercas Summit&lt;/a&gt; for panoramic views, or explore the Ruecas River Nature Trail. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6042271" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cancho del Fresno Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; is also a prominent feature, known for its recreational activities and scenic surroundings.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks accessible via hiking trails?
A: Yes, several trails lead to or pass by significant landmarks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1272865170/embalse-del-cancho-del-fresno-y-cueva-la-chiquita-circular-desde-canamero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse del Cancho del Fresno y Cueva la Chiquita — circular desde Cañamero&lt;/a&gt; passes near the historically significant &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4461596" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Chiquita Cave&lt;/a&gt;, known for prehistoric paintings. The challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990834687/guadalupe-viaduct-from-canamero-via-the-isabel-la-catolica-trail-pr-cc-242" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalupe Viaduct from Cañamero&lt;/a&gt; route leads towards the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3847287" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalupe Viaduct&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Cañamero?
A: The hiking trails around Cañamero are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 50 reviews. Hikers often praise the unique geological formations, the serene reservoir landscapes, and the opportunity to explore ancient river canyons within the Geopark.

Q: Is there a good time of year to visit Cañamero for hiking?
A: Cañamero, located in Extremadura, generally experiences hot summers. Spring and autumn offer more temperate weather, making them ideal seasons for hiking. The Mediterranean flora is vibrant in spring, and the autumn colors can be beautiful, providing comfortable conditions for exploring the trails.

Q: Are there any accessible paths for easier walks or cycling?
A: For those seeking flatter, more accessible routes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1813326" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde Vegas del Guadiana&lt;/a&gt; is a great option. Built on an old railway line, it offers a scenic path through wetlands and agricultural areas, crossing streams via old railway viaducts, suitable for walking or cycling.

Q: Where can I learn more about the geology of the Cañamero region?
A: Cañamero hosts a Visitor Reception Center for the Villuercas-Ibores-Jara UNESCO Global Geopark. Here, you can learn about the characteristic geological formations, fossils, and minerals through interactive exhibits and 3D videos, enhancing your understanding of the landscape you'll be hiking through.

Q: Are there any challenging long-distance hikes available?
A: Yes, for experienced hikers seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e990834687/guadalupe-viaduct-from-canamero-via-the-isabel-la-catolica-trail-pr-cc-242" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadalupe Viaduct from Cañamero via the Isabel la Católica Trail (PR-CC 242)&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 27.9 km path. This extensive route offers a demanding experience through the regional landscape, with significant elevation changes.
tips: It was the first cave with paintings discovered by science in the province of Cáceres. It's a place shrouded in legend about a snake and has been used as both a refuge and a ritual meeting place for centuries.
If you decide to explore this route, you will be surrounded by nature, history and breathtaking views. Enjoy your hike! 🏞️🚶‍♂️🥾🚵🌞

During the twenties and thirties of the last century, a railway route was approved that was to link Badajoz, Madrid and Huelva, passing through Villanueva de la Serena and Talavera de la Reina. The wild foothills of the Villuercas represented an obstacle that proved insurmountable and that caused the project to be abandoned three decades after its start. As a reminder of that ill-fated project, today we have a grandiose viaduct at the gates of Guadalupe, crossing the broken Guadalupejo valley from one end to the other.
